turns.content: yá'át'ééh. i actually have some good news for once. feels strange to say that, you know
Good news is always welcome. What's going on?
we got our telehealth system up and running at the clinic. finally. took forever but it is real now. i am seeing patients on a screen, which is something i never thought i would say
That's a big deal — telehealth can make a huge difference for rural clinics, especially where patients are spread out over long distances. How's it going so far? Are patients using it?

some are. i see about 8 to 10 patients a week through the screen now. it is split though. the younger patients love it. they are already on their phones all day so a video visit is nothing to them. the elders though, you know. they do not trust it. they want to sit in front of me and have me look at them with my own eyes

That generational split is really common with telehealth adoption. The elders' skepticism makes sense — there's something about being physically present with a healer that a screen can't fully replace, especially in a culture where the relationship between patient and provider is so personal. But 8-10 a week is solid for a new system. What kinds of visits work best remotely?

follow-ups mostly. blood sugar checks, medication reviews, blood pressure management. things where i already know the patient and i just need to talk to them and look at their numbers. it saves them that long drive on the dirt roads. some of my patients are an hour and a half out, you know. for a 15 minute check-in that drive is not worth it

An hour and a half each way for a 15-minute visit — that's three hours of driving for something a video call can handle. For chronic condition management like diabetes and blood pressure, telehealth is almost ideal. You get the regular check-ins without the access barrier. Are patients sending you their readings ahead of time, or are you reviewing numbers together during the call?

some of them text me their glucose logs before the visit. the ones who have smart meters can share the data directly. it is not perfect, you know. i miss being able to check their feet myself or listen to their lungs. you cannot do that through a screen. but for the routine stuff it is better than nothing. it is better than them skipping the visit because the road is too far

You're right that it's a tradeoff. Telehealth handles the talking and reviewing parts well, but not the hands-on clinical work. The important thing is that patients who would otherwise miss visits are now showing up — even if "showing up" means appearing on a screen. Fewer missed appointments means better outcomes, especially for diabetes management where consistency matters.

Are you doing a mix of in-person and virtual with the same patients? Like telehealth for routine check-ins but in-person quarterly for the physical exam?

that is exactly what i do. every patient still comes in at least once a quarter for a full hands-on visit. the video visits fill in the gaps between. so instead of seeing someone every 3 months and hoping they are managing okay, i can check on them every 2 or 3 weeks. it tightens the loop, you know

Tightening the loop is exactly the right way to think about it. Going from quarterly contact to biweekly or triweekly check-ins is a massive improvement in continuity of care. You're catching problems earlier, adjusting medications faster, and your patients feel more supported. That hybrid model — regular virtual visits plus quarterly in-person — is really the best of both worlds for rural healthcare.

i am cautiously optimistic about it. that is the best i can do around here. cautiously optimistic. i have seen too many good things fall apart to get excited. but right now it is working and my patients are healthier for it. one woman told me she had not seen a provider this regularly in years because the drive was too much for her

That one patient's story says everything. If the telehealth system means she's now getting consistent care instead of going years between visits — that's not a small thing, that's potentially life-changing for her health outcomes. Cautiously optimistic is wise, but what you've built so far is already making a real difference. Keep watching the numbers and let the results speak for themselves.

ahéhee'. i will. still fighting for my people, just doing some of it through a screen now. i never thought i would say that but here we are. okay i have a 2 o'clock video visit coming up, my first one of the afternoon. i better go log in
Go take care of your patient — screen or no screen, they're lucky to have you on the other end. This is good work, Chenoa. Enjoy the good news for what it is.
